title: Cornell University - Founding
text: Cornell University was founded in 1865 by Ezra Cornell and Andrew Dickson White. Its founding principle was to create an institution where any person could find instruction in any study. It was inaugurated on October 7, 1868, with 412 students.

title: Cornell University - Location
text: Cornell's main campus is located on East Hill in Ithaca, New York, overlooking the city and Cayuga Lake. The campus spans approximately 2,300 acres. Cornell also maintains facilities in New York City, Washington D.C., and Qatar.

title: Cornell University - Ivy League and Land Grant
text: Cornell is a member of the Ivy League and is the federal land-grant institution of New York State. It is unique among Ivy League schools in having both privately endowed and state-supported statutory colleges. This dual structure reflects its land-grant mission of public engagement.

title: Cornell University - Colleges and Schools
text: Cornell has fourteen colleges and schools, offering more than 80 undergraduate majors and over 120 graduate fields of study. These include the College of Arts and Sciences, the College of Engineering, the College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, and the Nolan School of Hotel Administration, among others.

title: Cornell University - Student Body
text: The university enrolls approximately 16,000 undergraduate students and over 10,000 graduate and professional students. The student body is supported by a faculty of roughly 3,000 members. Cornell distributed over $460 million in undergraduate grant aid during the 2025-2026 academic year.

title: Cornell University - Nobel Laureates
text: Cornell has produced 62 Nobel laureates affiliated with the university as faculty, alumni, or researchers. Notable Nobel-winning research at Cornell spans physics, chemistry, economics, and medicine. The university is consistently ranked among the top research institutions in the world.

title: Cornell University - Libraries
text: The Cornell University Library system is one of the largest academic libraries in the United States, with over 8 million volumes. The system includes multiple specialized libraries across campus. Olin Library and Uris Library are central landmarks on the Arts Quad.

title: Cornell University - Botanic Gardens
text: The Cornell Botanic Gardens encompass over 3,600 acres of natural areas and cultivated gardens. The F.R. Newman Arboretum features extensive collections of trees and shrubs. The gardens serve as both a teaching resource and a public green space.

title: Ithaca - Gorges and Waterfalls
text: Ithaca is famous for its dramatic gorges and waterfalls. Cascadilla Gorge runs through the center of the city, connecting the Cornell campus to downtown. Other notable gorges include Fall Creek Gorge on campus, featuring Ithaca Falls, the largest waterfall in the region.

title: Ithaca - Taughannock Falls
text: Taughannock Falls State Park, located just north of Ithaca, is home to Taughannock Falls. At 215 feet, it is the tallest single-drop waterfall east of the Rocky Mountains, taller than Niagara Falls. The park offers hiking trails, swimming, and cross-country skiing.

title: Ithaca - Climate and Weather
text: Ithaca has a humid continental climate with cold, snowy winters and warm summers. Average winter temperatures often drop below 20 degrees Fahrenheit, and the area receives about 65 inches of snow per year. The city is known for its long winters and its unofficial motto, 'Ithaca is Gorges.'

title: Ithaca - The Commons
text: The Ithaca Commons is a pedestrian mall in the heart of downtown Ithaca. It features a mix of locally owned shops, restaurants, and galleries. The Commons hosts regular events including festivals, concerts, and a weekly farmers market.

title: Ithaca - Dining and Food Culture
text: Ithaca has a vibrant food scene with a strong emphasis on farm-to-table dining and locally sourced ingredients. The city is home to the Ithaca Farmers Market at Steamboat Landing, which operates seasonally. The Finger Lakes wine region surrounding Ithaca features numerous wineries, breweries, and cideries along the Cayuga Lake Wine Trail.

title: Ithaca - Cayuga Lake
text: Cayuga Lake is the longest of the Finger Lakes, stretching about 40 miles. Its southern tip is adjacent to Ithaca, providing opportunities for boating, fishing, and swimming. The lake's deep waters remain cold even in summer, and it plays a central role in the region's wine-growing climate.

title: Ithaca - Educational Institutions
text: In addition to Cornell University, Ithaca is home to Ithaca College, located on South Hill with panoramic views of Cayuga Lake. The city's population roughly doubles when students are in session. Ithaca's identity is deeply shaped by its role as a college town.

title: Ithaca - Outdoor Recreation
text: Ithaca offers extensive outdoor recreation including the 8-mile Cayuga Waterfront Trail along the southern shore of Cayuga Lake and the 8.5-mile Black Diamond Trail. Buttermilk Falls State Park and Robert H. Treman State Park provide additional hiking, swimming, and camping opportunities.

title: Cornell University - Research
text: Cornell is classified as a research university with very high research activity. The university operates major research facilities including the Cornell High Energy Synchrotron Source and the Cornell NanoScale Science and Technology Facility. Annual research expenditures exceed one billion dollars.

title: Ithaca - Arts and Culture
text: Ithaca has a thriving arts and cultural scene. The city is home to the Hangar Theatre, Cinemapolis independent cinema, and the Herbert F. Johnson Museum of Art on the Cornell campus, designed by I.M. Pei. The Ithaca Festival, held annually in June, celebrates the community with music, art, and a parade.
